title: Managing a young ram's first season
subtitle: Preparing a ram lamb for tupping and watching his performance.
excerpt: A shearling ram put to ewes for the first time is physically capable of tupping but may lack the experience and confidence to cover a large number of ewes efficiently. Selecting the right ratio, monitoring his progress daily, and having a backup plan if he fails are the essentials of a first-time ram season.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb ram management guide, ahdb.org.uk. SAC Consulting sheep production notes. DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Sheep.

glossaryTerms.definition: A ram that has been shorn once — between one and two years old. Capable of tupping but not yet at his mature size or experience.
The period when a ram is run with ewes for mating. Typically five weeks in autumn, covering two full ewe cycles.
A coloured wax crayon or harness fitted to the ram's brisket. Marks ewes he has served. Change the colour after seventeen days to identify ewes served in the second cycle.

content.text: A
shearling ram
put to ewes for the first time needs a smaller flock than he could theoretically cover. A mature ram can serve twenty to forty ewes over a five-week
tupping

period. A shearling doing his first season should be given no more than fifteen ewes to start. His libido and technique are usually adequate; his stamina and confidence with difficult ewes may not be.
Pre-tupping checks

Six weeks before tupping, check the ram's feet, his teeth, and his body condition. He should be in good flesh but not fat, a body condition score of around 3.5 is the target. A ram in poor condition going into tupping will lose weight quickly and may become reluctant to serve by the second week.

Check the scrotum: both testicles present, firm and equal in size, no hard lumps or asymmetry. A ram with an abnormal testicle may be sub-fertile. Your vet can carry out a breeding soundness assessment if there is any doubt.
Fitting the raddle
Fit a
raddle
harness before putting the ram in with the ewes, see the
fitting a raddle on a tup

attrs.label: fitting a raddle on a tup

content.text: tutorial for the procedure. Start with a pale colour (yellow, orange). After seventeen days, change to a darker colour (red). Ewes marked with the second colour were not served in the first cycle and returned to oestrus.
Daily monitoring

Check the ram and ewes daily. In the first week, you should see raddle marks building on ewes, by day seven in a group of fifteen ewes you should have marks on most of them. If by day ten fewer than eight ewes are marked, the ram is not serving effectively.
A young ram can develop sore feet quickly from the repetitive mounting behaviour if the ground is hard or stony. Check his feet twice a week during tupping.
The
tupping
tutorial covers the full five-week process in detail.

A ram lamb (under twelve months) should not be used for tupping in his first autumn if he is small or in poor condition. He is likely to be outcompeted by the ewes and lose more weight than his growing frame can sustain. Either keep a shearling ram as a backup, or delay using the ram lamb until the following autumn.
After tupping

Remove the ram after five weeks. Weigh or condition-score him: a ram that has lost more than half a condition score unit needs building up before autumn shearing or the following tupping season. Separate from the ewes until you are sure pregnancy has been established, ewes returning to oestrus at six weeks after the ram is removed indicate he may have been infertile or the conception rate was low.
